coredata.dc:title: Effects of alkyl side-chain length on binding with bovine serum albumin, cytotoxicity, and antibacterial properties of 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ionic liquids
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Tingting
Wang, Xiaojuan
Shu, Yang
Wang, Jianhua
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the behavior of 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ionic liquids (ILs) with ethyl, butyl, hexyl, octyl, and decyl side-chains was evaluated to gain a deeper insight into the potential hazards posed by ILs to the environment and organisms. The effects of the alkyl side-chain length on the interactions between the ILs and bovine serum albumin (BSA), the cytotoxicity and antibacterial performance of the ILs was evaluated. The BSA binding ability of the ILs increased with increasing alkyl side-chain length, and the binding constants of the ILs increased from 1.80 L/mol for 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide to 196 L/mol for 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide. IL binding with BSA was a spontaneous process initiated by hydrophobic forces. This was confirmed using circular dichroism spectra, dynamic light scattering measurements and molecular docking simulations. The results of the cytotoxicity and antimicrobial activity of ILs demonstrated that the increase in alkyl side-chain length increased IL toxicity. The effects of the alkyl side-chain length of 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ILs on the interactions between ILs and BSA and cytotoxicity of the ILs were also evaluated. Furthermore, we demonstrated that tetrafluoroborate-based ILs were more toxic than dicyanamide-based ILs. Herein, we thoroughly describe the effects of the alkyl side-chain length on the properties of ILs and provide background information for future studies on the properties of carbon dots fabricated using ILs as precursors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ionic liquids
Interaction
(Bio)toxicity
Structure–activity relationship
Molecular docking

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Properties of CDs prepared using IL precursors

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21974018, 21727811, and 22074011), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( N2005015 , and N2005027 ), and Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC1907191, XLYC1802016).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of bovine serum albumin (BSA) (excitation wavelength of 280 nm) mixed with various concentrations of (a) 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (EmimDCN), (b) 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (BmimDCN), (c) 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (HmimDCN), (d) 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (OmimDCN), and (e) 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (DmimDCN) ionic liquids (ILs). (f) Stern–Volmer plots of dicyanamide IL-induced BSA fluorescence quenching. Experimental conditions: CILs = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 50, 75, 100, and 125 mM, CBSA = 10 μM, pH = 7.4, T = 298 K. The error bars indicate the standard deviations for three independent measurements.

Effects of phenylbutazone and ibuprofen site probes on the fluorescence of ionic liquid (IL)–bovine serum albumin (BSA)systems (a) 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide–BSA, (b) 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide–BSA, (c) 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide–BSA, (d) 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide OmimDCN–BSA, and (e) 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide DmimDCN–BSA systems. Experimental conditions: CILs = 1 mM, CBSA = 10 μM. CProbe = 0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 125 μM. The error bars indicate the standard deviations of three independent measurements.

Synchronous fluorescence spectra of ionic liquid (IL)–bovine serum albumin (BSA) systems with different concentrations of (a) 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (EmimDCN), (b) 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (BmimDCN), (c) 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (HmimDCN), (d) 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (OmimDCN), and (e) 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (DmimDCN) ILs. (A) 15 and (B) 60 nm. (C) Maximum emission peak changes. Experimental conditions: CBSA = 10 μM, pH = 7.4, T = 298 K. The error bars indicate the standard deviations of three independent measurements.

(A) Optimum docking structure and (B) molecular interactions of bovine serum albumin with (a) 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium (Emim), (b) 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium (Bmim), (c) 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium (Hmim), (d) 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium (Omim), and (e) 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium (Dmim) cations.

Cytotoxicity of (A) MCF-7 and (B) LOVO cells incubated with different concentration of (a) 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide, (b) 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide, (c) 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide, (d) 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide, (e) 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide, (f) 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate, (g) 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate, and (h) 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ionic liquids (ILs). The error bars indicate the standard deviations of three independent measurements.

Effect of concentration of (a) 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide, (b) 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide, (c) 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide, (d) 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide, and (e) 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ionic liquids on the growth of (A) Staphylococcus aureus and (B) Escherichia coli. The error bars indicate the standard deviations of three independent measurements.

Double-exponential fitting of BSA (10 μM) in the absence and presence of different concentration of 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (DmimDCN) decay curves. The average lifetime (τ) was calculated by the equation: τ = τ1 × A1 + τ2 × A2.

head.ce:title: Effects of alkyl side-chain length on binding with bovine serum albumin, cytotoxicity, and antibacterial properties of 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ionic liquids

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the behavior of 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ionic liquids (ILs) with ethyl, butyl, hexyl, octyl, and decyl side-chains was evaluated to gain a deeper insight into the potential hazards posed by ILs to the environment and organisms. The effects of the alkyl side-chain length on the interactions between the ILs and bovine serum albumin (BSA), the cytotoxicity and antibacterial performance of the ILs was evaluated. The BSA binding ability of the ILs increased with increasing alkyl side-chain length, and the binding constants of the ILs increased from 1.80 L/mol for 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide to 196 L/mol for 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide. IL binding with BSA was a spontaneous process initiated by hydrophobic forces. This was confirmed using circular dichroism spectra, dynamic light scattering measurements and molecular docking simulations. The results of the cytotoxicity and antimicrobial activity of ILs demonstrated that the increase in alkyl side-chain length increased IL toxicity. The effects of the alkyl side-chain length of 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ILs on the interactions between ILs and BSA and cytotoxicity of the ILs were also evaluated. Furthermore, we demonstrated that tetrafluoroborate-based ILs were more toxic than dicyanamide-based ILs. Herein, we thoroughly describe the effects of the alkyl side-chain length on the properties of ILs and provide background information for future studies on the properties of carbon dots fabricated using ILs as precursors.

ce:section.ce:para: Ionic liquids (ILs) are molten ionic compounds comprising different organic cations and organic/inorganic anions [1]. ILs have been used in separation [2], (bio)catalysis [3], electrochemistry [4], drug delivery [5], and other fields [6–8] because of their favorable characteristics, such as low vapor pressure, good solubility, high electrical conductivity, flexibility, and ability to change their ionic structure. Owing to the wide industrial use of ILs and their discharging into the environment, the potential toxicity and safety of ILs should be studied. Recently, the biological and environmental toxicity of ILs with different anions, cations, and alkyl side-chain lengths of the cation moieties have been studied in proteins, cells, tissues and organs, aquatic organisms, and plants [9–12].

The effects of cations on IL toxicity have been analyzed and reported by several researchers [9,13–15]. The toxicity of five types of cations on Vibrio cheri and Daphnia magna aquatic organisms decreased as follows: tetrazole > triazole > imidazole > pyridine > quaternary ammonium salts [13]. The effects of the alkyl side-chain length is important in test systems, from molecular to cellular level [9,14,15]. The binding/denaturation of bovine serum albumin (BSA) with 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate and the toxicity of imidazolium-based tetrafluoroborates for cells and organisms increased with increasing alkyl side-chain length [9,14]. Low-viscosity ammonium-based ILs with short alkyl side-chains resulted in stronger destabilization of heme proteins than ammonium-based ILs with long alkyl side-chains because the interactions between the alkyl side-chains and hydrophobic regions of the protein were stronger for the ammonium-based ILs with short alkyl side-chains [15].

An in-depth understanding of the interactions between ILs and biomolecules (e.g., peptides, nucleic acids, phospholipid bilayers, and proteins) is beneficial for the application of ILs in biological fields and can be used to explain IL toxicity [16]. The amino acid sequence of BSA, which is a type of globular protein and the main protein in bovine serum, is similar to that of human serum albumin [17]. BSA plays an important role in transporting substances and maintaining a stable osmotic pressure in blood. The interactions of ILs with BSA have been studied by many researchers [14,17–19]. The hydrophobic character of ILs with long alkyl side-chains necessary for effective IL–BSA interaction and the consequent denaturation of the BSA structure has been previously reported [14]. Because nitrate-containing imidazolium-based ILs interacted more strongly with BSA and disrupted the essential intra-BSA hydrogen bonds to a larger extent than chloride-containing ILs, they exerted a stronger influence on the conformation of BSA [17]. The hydrophilicity of pyrrolidinium acetate-based ILs contributed significantly to the stability of the molecular structure of BSA [18]. In addition, surface-active ILs, that is, fluorinated ILs have been reported to increase the stability of BSA [19].

To identify the types of potentially toxic and biohazardous ILs, in this study, the effects of 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ILs on interacting with BSA were analyzed by ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, circular dichroism spectra, fourier transform infrared spectra and dynamic light scattering measurements. The effects of the alkyl side-chain length on the interactions between BSA and ILs (binding mode, binding force, binding site, and conformation) were evaluated. Molecular docking simulation experiments were used to analyze the nature of the IL–BSA interaction(s). In addition, the cytotoxicity and antibacterial activity of ILs were studied to provide further insight into the effects of the alkyl side-chain length. We selected 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ILs as controls to compare the IL–BSA interactions and cytotoxicity with those of 1-alkyl-3-methylimiduzolium dicyanamide ILs.

BSA was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (EmimDCN), 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (BmimDCN), 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (HmimDCN), 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (OmimDCN), 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (DmimDCN), 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (EmimBF4), 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (HmimBF4), and 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (DmimBF4) ILs were purchased from Shanghai Cheng Jie Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM, high glucose), trypsin, penicillin, streptomycin, and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Thermo Scientific (Logan, UT, USA). Peptone and yeast extracts were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Shanghai, China). Nanjing KeyGEN Biotech (Nanjing, China) supplied 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay kits. Sodium chloride (NaCl), hydrochloric acid, sodium hydroxide, potassium chloride, disodium phosphate, potassium dihydrogen phosphate, and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phenylbutazone and ibuprofen were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Human breast cancer (MCF-7) and colon cancer (LOVO) cells were procured from the National Collection of Authenticated Cell Cultures (Shanghai, China). Staphylococcus aureus (CMCC (B) 26003) bacteria were purchased from the National Center for Medical Culture Collections (Beijing, China). Escherichia coli (DH5α) bacteria were acquired from Beijing Fenghui Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All reagents were of analytical reagent grade and were used as received. Deionized water (18 MΩ/cm) was used throughout the experiments.

First, a 20 μM BSA solution and 1 M 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide solutions in phosphate buffer solution (PBS; 0.01 M, pH 7.4) were prepared. As for the tetrafluoroborate-based ILs, 1 M EmimBF4, 200 mM HmimBF4, and 100 mM DmimBF4 solutions were prepared using PBS as the solvent. Equal volumes of IL solutions of different concentrations and BSA solution (20 μM) were added to 2-mL microtubes and mixed for at least 20 min before spectroscopy measurements.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) at 298 K using 1-cm long cells at a scan speed of 2400 nm/min. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) using 5-mm long cells and a 5-nm slit at a scan speed of 2400 nm/min and scanning lamp voltage of 530 V. Fluorescence lifetime assays were measured on an FLS920 steady–transient state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instrument, UK) using 1-cm long cells. Circular dichroism (CD) spectra were collected using a MOS-450 (Bio-Logic, France) automatic recording spectropolarimeter at 298 K. The spectra were recorded using 1-mm long cells and an acquisition period of 0.05 s, and the results were expressed as CD millidegrees. Attenuated total reflection Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Tensor II (Bruker, Germany) FTIR spectrometer in the wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were conducted on a Zetasizer Nano S90 analyzer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK) using 1-cm long cells. The samples were thermally equilibrated for 120 s before each measurement, and the averages of 30 measurement runs were reported. All tests were repeated at least three times.

Molecular docking simulations were performed using the AutoDock Vina software and the Lamarckian genetic algorithm method [20]. The crystal structure of BSA was retrieved from the RCSB Protein Data Bank (http://www.rcsb.org/pdb, DOI: https://doi.org//10.2210/pdb4F5S/pdb, 4F5S for BSA). The structure of BSA was optimized before molecular docking using the PyMOL 2.4.1 software. The structures of the IL cations were modeled using the ChemDraw software and optimized using the Chem 3D 19.0 software. Both initial ligand molecules had arbitrary conformation orientations and positions. A 100 Å × 70 Å × 80 Å grid box with a 1 Å spacing between grid points was used for docking. The docked conformation of the BSA–ligand complexes was subsequently analyzed using the PyMOL 2.4.1 and Discovery Studio Visualizer software. The most favorable docking model was selected based on binding energy and matching geometry.

The cytotoxicity of the IL systems was analyzed using a standard MTT assay. First, 100 μL of a MCF-7 or a LOVO cell suspension (approximately 104 cells) was added to each well of a 96-well plate, followed by preculturing for 24 h under standard culture conditions (DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin, 37 °C, 5% CO2). After the culture medium was removed, the cells were incubated for 24 h in DMEM containing various IL concentrations. Next, the culture medium was removed, and the wells were washed twice with PBS. Thereafter, 100 μL of fresh culture medium and 10 μL of a 5 mg/mL MTT solution were added to each well, followed by incubation for 4 h. Subsequently, the culture medium was removed, and 100 μL of DMSO was added to each well. The mixtures were shaken for 10 min at 298 K, and their absorbance at 490 nm was recorded using a Synergy H1 Microplate reader (Biotek, USA). Each cytotoxicity assay was performed in triplicate.

The antibacterial activity of the 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ILs was examined using S. aureus gram-positive bacteria and E. coli gram-negative bacteria cultured in Luria–Bertani medium. First, 100 μL of 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ILs was added to a well of a 96-well plate and was double diluted. Thereafter, 100 μL of bacterial solution was added to each well. The initial bacteria concentration was adjusted to 0.1 of the optical density at 600 nm (OD600). The plate was incubated at 37 °C for 12 h and the OD600 values of the mixtures in each well were measured at 2-h intervals using a Synergy H1 Microplate reader (Biotek, USA). Each antimicrobial assay was performed in triplicate.

Previous studies demonstrated that the imidazolium moieties of imidazolium-based ILs affected the conformation of BSA [14,19,21]. However, the dicyanamide anion has not been studied sufficiently because of the diversity of ions comprising ILs. In this study, imidazolium-based ILs with different alkyl side-chains (EmimDCN, BmimDCN, HmimDCN, OmimDCN, and DmimDCN) were analyzed. The ILs possessed the same anionic moiety [N(CN)2−] and cationic imidazolium counterparts with ethyl, butyl, hexyl, octyl, and decyl side-chains. In addition, three imidazolium tetrafluoroborate ILs with ethyl, hexyl, and decyl alkyl side-chains (EmimBF4, HmimBF4, and DmimBF4, respectively) were selected as controls. The structures of all the ILs are presented in Table S1.

The distinct and strong absorption band at 225 nm in the UV–vis spectrum of BSA (Fig. S1a) was attributed to the π–π* transition of the typical C = O polypeptide backbone structure [22], whereas the absorption peak at 280 nm was assigned to the n–π* transition of phenylalanine (Phe), tryptophan (Trp), and tyrosine (Tyr) aromatic amino acids [23], with a predominant contribution from Trp. Therefore, the band centered at 280 nm in the UV–vis spectrum of BSA can provide information on the Trp residues of BSA when other molecules bind to them. No absorption peaks were observed at 280 nm in the UV–vis spectra of single 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide IL solutions (Fig. S1a). The intensity of the absorption band of BSA in the IL–BSA solutions changed (Fig. S1b). This indicated that the interactions between ILs and BSA affected the microenvironment of the amino acid residues of BSA. The intensity of the BSA peak at 280 nm increased gradually with increasing alkyl side-chain length of the imidazolium cation of the ILs. In addition, the peak centered at 280 nm blue-shifted. With increasing alkyl side-chain length of the imidazolium cation of the ILs, the increase of the intensity indicated the decrease of the helix in BSA and the elongation of the BSA structure. The blue-shifted peak of BSA indicated the increase of the polarity around the aromatic amino acid residues. These results suggested that the changes in the microenvironment surrounding the aromatic amino acid residues of BSA are more pronounced with increasing alkyl side-chain length of the imidazolium cation of the ILs.

To further analyze the interactions of ILs with BSA, fluorescence spectroscopy was used to monitor the intrinsic BSA fluorescence. Aromatic amino acids, such as Trp and Tyr, are the main contributors to the intrinsic fluorescence of BSA [24]. In this study, the fluorescence peak at the excitation/emission wavelengths of 280/340 nm was used to provide information on the changes in the Trp and Tyr residues. The inner filter effect on fluorescence emission was ignored in this study because the dicyanamide-based ILs did not absorb light at the BSA excitation wavelength (280 nm) obtained from Fig. S1a.

The fluorescence spectra of BSA mixed with IL solutions of various concentrations (Fig. 1 a–e) revealed that the EmimDCN, BmimDCN, HmimDCN, OmimDCN, and DmimDCN ILs quenched the fluorescence of BSA at 298 K, and quenching efficiency increased with increasing IL concentration. The fluorescence spectra of single ILs excited at 280 nm did not include a distinct emission peak at approximately 340 nm (Fig. S2a). Therefore, the interference of ILs can be eliminated by studying the fluorescence of the IL–BSA systems. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching capability of the 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ILs increased with increasing alkyl side-chain length. Similar data were reported for ILs containing other anions, such as tetrafluoroborate [14] and chloride [25]. Therefore, 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ILs were selected as controls to further evaluate the effect of the alkyl side-chain length. The fluorescence quenching of BSA increased with increasing alkyl side-chain length of 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ILs (Fig. S3). In addition to an increase in fluorescence quenching with increasing OmimDCN and DmimDCN concentrations, the maximum emission peak of BSA blue-shifted with increasing OmimDCN and DmimDCN concentrations (Fig. S2b). DmimDCN caused an intense shift in the fluorescence emission spectrum of BSA at a lower concentration than OmimDCN. The shift in emission peak indicated that the amino acid residues of BSA were exposed to a more hydrophobic microenvironment and, subsequently, the presence of ILs stimulated conformational changes in BSA [21,26,27]. When the concentrations of OmimDCN and DmimDCN were greater than 75 and 15 mM, respectively, the changes in fluorescence intensity and maximum emission peaks of BSA were negligible. This can be explained that ILs expand and denature BSA at concentrations higher than a certain threshold [14]. These results indicated that the increase in alkyl side-chain length of ILs greatly affect the microenvironment of BSA, which was consistent with the UV–vis spectroscopy results.

The quenching of molecular fluorescence involves static and dynamic mechanisms, energy transfer, and excited-state reactions [14,21]. Typically, static quenching is associated with the formation of a ground-state complex between the fluorophore and the quencher, whereas dynamic quenching involves collisions between the fluorophore and the quencher. The classical Stern–Volmer (SV) equation was used to analyze BSA fluorescence quenching [28]:
(1)
F 0
F
\; = \;1\; + \;
K SV
Q
\; = \;1\; + \;
K q
τ 0
Q

where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of BSA in the absence and presence of ILs, respectively, [Q] is the concentration of ILs, KSV is the SV quenching constant, Kq is the bimolecular quenching constant, and τ0 is approximately 10−8 s, which is the average lifetime of biomacromolecules in the absence of quenchers (ILs).

The SV curves changed with increasing IL concentration at 298 K (Fig. 1f). The SV plots of EmimDCN-, BmimDCN-, and HmimDCN-induced BSA fluorescence quenching remained almost unchanged at IL concentrations lower than 25 mM, and concentrations in the range of 25–125 mM were selected for further analysis. The concentrations of OmimDCN and DmimDCN in the SV plots ranged between 5 and 25 mM and 1–5 mM, respectively, because the SV slopes were maximum in these concentration ranges. The SV plots of the IL-induced intrinsic BSA fluorescence quenching were linear at 298, 304, and 310 K (Fig. S4). The KSV and Kq values of the IL–BSA systems are summarized in Table S2. At 298 K, the KSV values increased as follows: EmimDCN (2.07 L/mol) < BmimDCN (5.59 L/mol) < HmimDCN (8.28 L/mol) < OmimDCN (36.1 L/mol) < DmimDCN (188 L/mol). These values suggested that the interactions between the 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ILs and BSA increased with increasing alkyl side-chain length of the imidazolium cation. For comparison, the KSV values of the 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate IL–BSA systems also increased with increasing alkyl side-chain length, as follows: EmimBF4 (2.59 L/mol) < HmimBF4 (9.52 L/mol) < DmimBF4 (249 L/mol). The KSV values of the IL–BSA systems comprising tetrafluoroborate-based ILs were larger than those of the IL–BSA systems comprising dicyanamide-based ILs for the same alkyl side-chain lengths.

Static and dynamic quenching can be distinguished based on their temperature-dependent behaviors [29]. The quenching efficiencies of the dynamic and static quenching modes increase and decrease, respectively, with increasing temperature. The KSV values of different IL–BSA systems followed a similar increasing trend with increasing temperature from 298 to 310 K (Table S2). An increase in KSV values indicated that dynamic quenching occurred during the interaction of ILs with BSA. It was also determined that the Kq values were smaller than the maximum bimolecular quenching constant of 2 × 1010 L/(mol s), indicating that dynamic quenching occurred during the interaction of ILs with BSA; otherwise, static quenching occurred [19,30]. The Kq values of the EmimDCN–BSA, BmimDCN–BSA, HmimDCN–BSA, and OmimDCN–BSA systems (Table S2) were smaller than the maximum bimolecular quenching constant at 298, 304, and 310 K, indicating that dynamic quenching dominated the interactions between ILs and BSA. However, the Kq value for the DmimDCN–BSA interaction was greater than the maximum bimolecular quenching constant at 304 and 310 K. This suggested that static quenching can be partly attributed to DmimDCN-induced BSA quenching with increasing temperature. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime experiments were used to furhter analyze DmimDCN-induced BSA quenching mode because of the Kq value for the DmimDCN–BSA interaction is very close to maximum bimolecular quenching constant. The results are displayed in Table 1 and Fig. S5. The average lifetime of BSA was 6.17 ns (χ2 = 1.058) with lifetimes of τ1 = 4.61 ns (44.0%) and τ2 = 7.40 ns (56.0%). In the presence of 1 and 5 mM DmimDCN, the average fluorescence lifetimes of BSA decreased to 5.50 ns and 4.82 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime results confirmed that dynamic quenching occurs in DmimDCN-induced BSA quenching. Overall, it was concluded that dynamic quenching was the main mechanism for IL-induced intrinsic BSA fluorescence quenching.

In our previous study, 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ILs were used to prepare hydrophilic and hydrophobic carbon dots (HCDs and OCDs, respectively) [31,32]. Such IL–carbon dot (IL–CD) systems also quenched the intrinsic fluorescence of BSA; however, the binding interactions of the IL–CD systems with BSA decreased with increasing alkyl side-chain length of the imidazolium cations of the precursor ILs. Moreover, the decrease in KSV values with increasing temperature indicated that the primary quenching mechanism for all the CD–BSA systems was static quenching. The different quenching modes of CDs than that of ILs were attributed to the nanometer size and surface groups of the CDs.

For the binding of ILs with BSA, the equilibrium between the free and bound ILs was evaluated using the following equation [33]:
(2)
lg
F 0
- F
F
\; = \;n\;lg
K a
- n\;lg
1
Q
-
(
F 0
- F)\;[
P t
]
F 0
where Ka is the binding constant, n is the average number of binding sites, and [Pt] is the concentration of BSA.

The Ka values of the IL–BSA systems and the n values of BSA at different temperatures are summarized in Table S3. The Ka values ranged between 1.80 and 196 L/mol and were smaller than the high binding affinities (106–108 L/mol) reported in the literature [34]. The Ka values in this study indicated that the binding capabilities of the ILs for BSA were relatively low. The increasing Ka values of the IL–BSA systems with increasing alkyl side-chain length indicated that the binding affinities of the 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ILs for BSA increased with increasing alkyl side-chain length of the imidazolium cation. At 298 K, the average n value was approximately 1.0, indicating that the IL-to-BSA molar ratio of the IL–BSA systems was 1:1. The binding affinities of 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ILs for BSA also increased with increasing alkyl side-chain length. For the same cation, the Ka values of the tetrafluoroborate-based ILs were larger than those of the dicyanamide-based ILs. Therefore, it was concluded that the binding affinities of the tetrafluoroborate-based ILs were higher than those of the dicyanamide-based ILs. Furthermore, the binding affinities of the reported HCDs and OCDs prepared using 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ILs as precursors [31,32] to BSA were higher than those of the precursor ILs because the Ka values of CDs (103–105 L/mol) were significantly higher than those of the corresponding precursor ILs.

The binding forces between ILs and BSA typically include hydrogen bonds and van der Waals, hydrophobic, and electrostatic forces. The thermodynamic parameters of the van’t Hoff equation were used to determine the binding forces [31]:
(3)
1 n
K a
= -
Δ H
TR
+
Δ S
R
(4)
Δ
G\; = \;
Δ
H\; - \;T
Δ S
where ΔH, ΔS, and ΔG are the enthalpy changes, entropy changes, and Gibbs free energy, T is the temperature (K), and R is the gas constant (8.314 J/(mol K)).

The thermodynamic parameters of the IL–BSA systems are listed in Table S3. The negative ΔG values indicated that the ILs bind to BSA spontaneously. The ΔH and ΔS values of all IL–BSA systems were positive, suggesting that the interactions between ILs and BSA may be driven by hydrophobic and electrostatic forces. This was different from the hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces between BSA and tetrafluoroborate-based ILs [14]. Because BSA is negatively charged under physiological conditions (pH 7.4) [35], electrostatic attraction can occur between imidazolium cations and BSA. The increase in ionic strength of the IL–BSA systems can weaken the electrostatic interactions. For all the IL–BSA systems, the fluorescence intensity remained virtually unchanged with increasing NaCl concentration in the range of 0–0.5 M (Fig. S6). These results indicated that the contributions of the electrostatic interactions to the interactions between ILs and BSA were small. Therefore, hydrophobic forces were the main force between BSA and the imidazolium-based ILs.

Competitive site displacement experiments can provide more information about the IL binding sites on BSA. Two types of site probes, namely phenylbutazone (site I) and ibuprofen (site II), were used for these experiments. The displacement of ILs by phenylbutazone and ibuprofen can cause significant changes in the fluorescence spectrum of BSA. Probe displacement was calculated as follows [36]:
(5)
Probe
d i s p l a c e m e n t
( % )
=
F 2
/
F 1
× 100 %
where F1 and F2 are the fluorescence of BSA in IL–BSA systems in the absence and presence of site probes.

The addition of phenylbutazone to the IL–BSA systems caused lower values of the probe displacements than the addition of ibuprofen (Fig. 2 ). This suggested that phenylbutazone displaced more ILs from the binding sites of BSA than ibuprofen. Therefore, it was concluded that ILs mainly attached to sites I of BSA, which are close to the Trp-213 residue.

Synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy is a critical method for evaluating changes in protein molecular conformation and the microenvironment of amino acid residues through the shift of the maximum emission peak [37]. The fluorescence emission of BSA originates mainly from its Trp and Tyr residues. In this study, the synchronous fluorescence of Tyr and Trp was measured at 15 and 60 nm, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of BSA at 60 nm was significantly higher than that at 15 nm (Fig. 3 ). The fluorescence intensity of Trp decreased gradually with increasing concentration, and changes in emission wavelength were also observed. The band at 60 nm blue-shifted in the presence of EmimDCN and DmimDCN (Fig. 3C). This suggested that EmimDCN and DmimDCN induced a hydrophobic environment around the Trp residues of BSA [38]. The red-shift of the band at 60 nm in the presence of BmimDCN, HmimDCN, and OmimDCN indicated that dicyanamide-based ILs created a polar environment around the Trp residues of BSA [37]. These results indicated that the interactions between ILs and BSA significantly affected the microenvironment of the Trp residues. The maximum emission wavelength of 15 nm, which corresponded to the Tyr residue, remained virtually unchanged, suggesting that the effect of the interactions between ILs and BSA on the microenvironment of Tyr was negligible. These observations indicated that the binding sites of the dicyanamide-based ILs were closer to the Trp residues of BSA than to the Tyr residue, which is consistent with the results of the site displacement experiments.

To evaluate the structural and conformational changes induced by dicyanamide-based ILs in BSA, the CD spectra of BSA were obtained in the absence and presence of ILs. The typical negative absorption bands of the α-helical structure of BSA were observed at 208 and 222 nm [37]. The CD spectra of native BSA presented a distinct negative peak at 222 nm and a small peak at 208 nm (Fig. S7f). At low IL concentrations (0.1, 0.2, and 0.5 mM), the position of the 222 nm peak of BSA did not change; however, the minimum values fluctuated (Fig. S7a–e). Upon increasing the concentration of ILs from 1 to 20 mM, the minimum value (222 nm) increased and its position red-shifted. These results indicated that the α-helix content of BSA decrease with increasing IL concentration. This was attributed to IL binding causing the expansion of the peptide structure of BSA, which caused the exposure of hydrophobic cavities and the disruption of microstructures around the aromatic amino acid residues. BSA unfolding led to the exposure of more binding sites and a decrease in its α-helix content [17].

The nature of BSA was confirmed using FTIR spectroscopy, which is an invasive method used to study structural and conformational changes in protein structure. The presence of an amide I band at 1600–1700 cm−1 was helpful for analyzing the BSA structure. Different conformational structures of proteins can be identified at different wavenumbers as follows: intermolecular β-sheet (1610–1630 cm−1, 1680–1692 cm−1), native β-sheet (1630–1643 cm−1), random coil (1638–1648 cm−1), α-helix (1650–1660 cm−1), turn (1660–1680 cm−1) [39]. Characteristic peaks of intermolecular β-sheet (~1624 and 1687 cm−1), β-sheet (~1641 cm−1), α-helix (~1653 cm−1), and turn structures (~1672 cm−1) were observed in the second derivative FTIR spectrum of pure BSA (Fig. S8a). A significant increase in the fraction of intermolecular β-sheet structures (~1624 cm−1) was observed in the second derivative FTIR spectra of BSA in the presence of different concentrations of dicyanamide-based ILs (Fig. S8b–f), indicating that the presence of dicyanamide-based ILs caused the structure of BSA to become looser and more extended.

DLS can be used for real-time monitoring of the changes in BSA size in solution. The intensity-weighted size distributions of the dicyanamide-based ILs are illustrated in Fig. S9. The behavior of all the ILs was almost identical, and the hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) of the IL–BSA solutions increased with increasing IL concentration. The increase in Dh can contribute to the unfolding of the secondary structure of BSA induced by ILs via hydrophobic and electrostatic forces [39,40]. Electrostatic attraction was eliminated by increasing the NaCl concentration. These results and the aforementioned thermodynamic parameters indicated that hydrophobic forces were the primary forces between BSA and the ILs in this study.

Molecular docking calculations using the Autodock method have helped gain more insight into the IL–BSA interactions. BSA presents multiple binding sites for 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium ILs [25]. The optimum energy binding sites of all imidazolium cationic moieties docked with BSA are illustrated in Fig. 4 A. The Emim cation entered subdomain IIIA. However, the other cations, namely Bmim, Hmim, Omim, and Dmim interacted with BSA at subdomain IIIB. The docking binding affinity of imidazolium cations with longer alkyl side-chains (Table S1) was more negative than that of imidazolium cations with shorter alkyl side-chains, further implying that the binding ability of imidazolium-based ILs with BSA increased sequentially with increasing alkyl side-chain length. This was consistent with the conclusions of the aforementioned spectroscopy experiments. The molecular interactions between the imidazolium cations and BSA are presented in Fig. 4B. C–H bonds and alkyl interactions occurred between the Emim cations and isoleucine (Ile)387, valine (Val)432, and leucine (Leu)452 residues. Alkyl, π–alkyl, and π–σ interactions occurred between the Bmim cations and the phenylalanine (Phe)506, Phe508, alanine (Ala)527, and Phe550 residues of BSA and between the Hmim cations and the Phe501, Phe506, Leu531, Leu574, and Val575 residues of BSA. C–H bonds, alkyl, π–alkyl, and π–σ interactions were observed between the Omim cations and the Phe501, Phe506, lysine (Lys)524, Ala527, Leu531, Phe550, and Val575 residues of BSA. Alkyl, π–alkyl, and π–σ interactions occurred between the Dmim cations and the Phe506, Leu528, Leu531, Phe550, Leu574, and Val575 residues of BSA. These results indicated that alkyl interactions played an important role in the interactions between the amino acid residues of BSA and the alkyl side-chains of the imidazolium cations, confirming that hydrophobic interactions occurred between BSA and the ILs. Hence, the spectroscopy and molecular docking results confirmed that hydrophobic forces were the main forces between ILs and BSA.

Owing to the widespread applications of ILs, evaluating their toxicity is critical. In this study, to assess the toxicity of 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ILs, their cytotoxicity and antibacterial properties were evaluated, and the structure–bioactivity relationship of ILs was analyzed.

The cytotoxicity of 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ILs was evaluated by testing them against MCF-7 and LOVO cells using a standard MTT assay. Imidazolium-based ILs can induce cell apoptosis by damaging the cellular membrane structure, decreasing the mitochondrial membrane potential, and causing oxidative damage [41,42]. The toxicity of imidazolium-based ILs increased with increasing alkyl side-chain length probably owing to an increase in membrane affinity causing increased membrane permeability and oxidative stress [43,44]. As the alkyl side-chain length of dicyanamide-based ILs increased, the cytotoxicity of the ILs for MCF-7 cells after 24 h of incubation increased (Fig. 5 A a-e). Moreover, the calculated inhibitory concentrations at half maximum (IC50) of EmimDCN, BmimDCN, HmimDCN, OmimDCN, DmimDCN, were 26.9, 17.3, 1.75, 0.259, 0.0919 mM, respectively. Similar results were obtained for LOVO cells (Fig. 5B); that is, the cytotoxicity of dicyanamide-based ILs increased gradually with increasing alkyl side-chain length. The cytotoxicity of 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ILs for MCF-7 cells was evaluated as a control experiment (Fig. 5A f-h). As the alkyl side-chain length of the tetrafluoroborate-based ILs increased, the viability of the MCF-7 cells decreased. This further demonstrated that ILs with longer alkyl side-chains were more toxic to cells. The calculated IC50 values of EmimBF4, HmimBF4, and DmimBF4 were 3.42, 0.340, and 0.0626 mM, respectively. The IC50 values of the tetrafluoroborate-based ILs were lower than those of the dicyanamide-based ILs with the same cations. Therefore, the tetrafluoroborate-based ILs were more toxic to MCF-7 cells than the dicyanamide-based ILs. Furthermore, the MCF-7 cytotoxicity of the reported HCDs and OCDs prepared using 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ILs as precursors [31,32] were similar to those of the precursor ILs. As the alkyl side-chain length of the precursor dicyanamide -based ILs increased, the viability of the MCF-7 cells incubated with HCDs and OCDs also decreased. Moreover, OCDs displayed stronger cytotoxic than HCDs.

The antibacterial activity of 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ILs was tested against the gram-positive S. aureus and gram-negative E. coli bacteria. Imidazolium-based ILs can kill bacteria by inserting their hydrophobic moieties into cell membranes, causing cell walls to rupture and and disrupting normal functions, such as permeability and respiration [45,46]. Therefore, the antibacterial activity of the dicyanamide-based ILs was evaluated. The ILs with longer alkyl side-chains were more sensitive toward S. aureus and E. coli than those with shorter alkyl side-chains; thus, their inhibition concentrations were lower than those of the ILs with shorter alkyl side-chains (Fig. 6 ). The minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) of EmimDCN, BmimDCN, HmimDCN, OmimDCN, and DmimDCN for S. aureus were 150, 100, 12.5, 2.5, and 0.25 mM, respectively, and those for E. coli were 150, 100, 12.5, 5, and 0.5 mM, respectively. The MICs of 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ILs against bacteria decreased with increasing alkyl side-chain length, as previously reported [47]. ILs with long alkyl side-chains can permeabilize bacteria more easily than ILs with short side-chains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of CDs prepared using IL precursors
ce:section.ce:para: The interactions of HCDs and OCDs prepared using 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide IL precursors [31,32] with BSA and the cytotoxicity of HCDs and OCDs were evaluated. The length of the alkyl side-chains of the precursor ILs affected the structure, optical performance, and cytotoxicity of HCDs and OCDs. The hydrophilicity of the HCDs prepared using EmimDCN, BmimDCN, HmimDCN, OmimDCN, and DmimDCN IL precursors decreased, and their N doping amount decreased from 35.31% to 21.48%. Conversely, the numbers of C–O–C and –NH2 surface groups increased, and the quantum yield (QY) increased from 4.6% to 48.0%. The hydrophobicity and QYs of the corresponding five OCDs were similar, whereas the N doping amount decreased from 40.71% to 20.75%. The CD-induced BSA fluorescence quenching decreased with increasing alkyl side-chain length of the imidazolium cations, unlike that of the precursor ILs. Static quenching was the primary binding mechanism for the formation of CD–BSA complexes. In addition, the cytotoxicity of the CDs increased with increasing alkyl side-chain length of the precursor ILs, which was consistent with the behavior of the ILs.

The molecular- and cellular-level toxicities of the 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ILs with different alkyl side-chain lengths were compared. The alkyl side-chain length played an important role in the properties and behavior of the 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ILs. As the alkyl side-chain length of the imidazolium cations increased, the imidazolium-based ILs (1) presented stronger binding affinity with BSA and caused greater damage to it through hydrophobic forces, (2) presented higher cytotoxicity for MCF-7 and LOVO cells, and (3) exhibited increased antibacterial activity against gram-positive S. aureus and gram-negative E. coli bacteria. Molecular docking experiments indicated that ILs with longer alkyl side-chains were prone to binding with BSA via hydrophobic forces. The effects of the alkyl side-chain length of the 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ILs used as controls on the IL–BSA interactions and cytotoxicity of the ILs for MCF-7 cells were similar to those of the 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ILs. In addition, the tetrafluoroborate anion affected IL binding on BSA and cytotoxicity more than the dicyanamide anion. Herein we provide information on the effect of the alkyl side-chain length on the performance of ILs and background information for future studies on the properties of CDs fabricated using ILs as precursors.

Tingting Guo: Investigation, Data curation, Validation, Writing - original draft. Xiaojuan Wang: Investigation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Yang Shu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Jianhua Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21974018, 21727811, and 22074011), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N2005015, and N2005027), and Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC1907191, XLYC1802016).
We would like to thank Editage (www.editage.cn) for English language editing.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2021.116835.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
